levels.title: Identification & Form
levels.miniLesson:
🎯 Level 1: The Noun-Linker

A relative clause describes a noun that comes right before it. We use different relative pronouns depending on what kind of noun we are describing.

  • 👤 WHO: For People (The man who lives there).
  • 📦 WHICH: For Things/Animals (The car which I bought).
  • 🔗 THAT: For People or Things (Common in speech).
  • 📍 WHERE: For Places (The city where I live).
  • 🔑 WHOSE: For Possession (The girl whose phone rang).

questionBank.question: Choose the correct pronoun for a person: 'The scientist ___ won the award is very famous.'
questionBank.explanation: We use 'who' to refer to people.

questionBank.question: Which sentence correctly describes an object?
options: This is the bag who I bought.
This is the bag which I bought.
This is the bag where I bought.
This is the bag whose I bought.
questionBank.explanation: 'Which' is used for objects and things.

questionBank.question: Identify the relative pronoun for a location: 'That is the office ___ my father works.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Where' refers to a place.

questionBank.question: Complete the sentence: 'The book ___ I borrowed is very long.'
questionBank.explanation: 'That' can be used for things in defining clauses.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The doctor which treated me was very kind.'
questionBank.explanation: For a person, use 'who' or 'that'.

questionBank.question: Identify the possessive pronoun: 'The student ___ laptop is broken needs help.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Whose' shows possession (the student's laptop).

questionBank.question: Which pronoun is used for animals? 'The dog ___ was barking is my neighbor's.'
questionBank.explanation: Animals usually take 'which' or 'that' unless they are personified pets.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The hotel who we stayed in was clean.'
questionBank.explanation: A hotel is a place/thing; use 'which' or 'that'.

questionBank.question: Complete the sentence: 'The reason ___ I am late is the snow.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Why' is used after the noun 'reason'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct pronoun: 'The people ___ live in this city are friendly.'
questionBank.explanation: Refers to people.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'I lost the watch who you gave me.'
questionBank.explanation: Watch is a thing; use 'which' or 'that'.

questionBank.question: Which fits a place? 'I want to visit the town ___ my mother was born.'
questionBank.explanation: Refers to a location.

questionBank.question: Identify the possessive: 'The car ___ window is broken belongs to me.'
questionBank.explanation: The window of the car (possession).

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The singer which I like is on TV.'
questionBank.explanation: Singer is a person; use 'who' or 'that'.

questionBank.question: Complete: 'The email ___ I sent you had a mistake.'
questionBank.explanation: Email is a thing.

questionBank.question: Which is correct? 'The woman ___ bag was stolen called the police.'
questionBank.explanation: The bag belonging to the woman.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'This is the restaurant who has the best pizza.'
questionBank.explanation: Restaurant is a thing/place; use 'that' or 'which'.

questionBank.question: Complete the sentence: 'The time ___ I arrived was 10 PM.'
questionBank.explanation: 'When' is used for time periods.

questionBank.question: Which pronoun fits? 'The flowers ___ are in the vase are dying.'
questionBank.explanation: Things (flowers).

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The mountain who we climbed was very high.'
questionBank.explanation: Mountain is a thing; use 'which' or 'that'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct possessive: 'The company ___ office is in London is huge.'
questionBank.explanation: The office of the company.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The students which are in my class are smart.'
questionBank.explanation: Students are people; use 'who'.

questionBank.question: Complete: 'This is the house ___ I grew up.'
questionBank.explanation: Place/Location.

questionBank.question: Which pronoun fits? 'The teacher ___ gave us the homework was nice.'
questionBank.explanation: Teacher is a person.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The report who I finished is on the desk.'
questionBank.explanation: Report is a thing; use 'which' or 'that'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct possessive: 'A person ___ name I forgot called me.'
questionBank.explanation: The person's name.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The car which its engine is loud is old.'
questionBank.explanation: Redundant. Use 'The car whose engine is loud'.

questionBank.question: Complete: 'The museum ___ we visited was closed.'
questionBank.explanation: Referring to the museum as an object we visited.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The girl who she lives there is my friend.'
questionBank.explanation: Redundant subject. Should be 'The girl who lives there'.

questionBank.question: Final Check: A relative pronoun always comes ___ the noun it describes.
questionBank.explanation: In English syntax, the modifier follows the noun (antecedent).

levels.title: L1 Traps: The 'What' vs. 'That' Collision
levels.miniLesson:
⚠️ Level 2: The 'Greedy' Pronoun

In Japanese, you can use the same structure to modify a noun as you do to create a 'thing' clause. In English, WHAT is a 'greedy' pronoun—it already contains the noun 'thing' inside it.

  • ❌ L1 Trap: I liked the book what you gave me. (Error: You have two nouns in one spot).
  • ✅ Correct: I liked the book that you gave me.
  • ✅ Correct: I liked what you gave me. (No 'book' before it).
  • 💡 The Golden Rule: If there is a Noun (car, man, idea) before the gap, NEVER use 'what'. Use 'that' or 'which'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct pronoun: 'This is the evidence ___ the committee requested.'
questionBank.explanation: Since 'evidence' is a noun, you cannot use 'what'. Use 'that' or 'which'.

questionBank.question: Which sentence correctly uses 'what'?
options: I don't understand the reason what he said.
I don't understand what he said.
I don't understand the words what he said.
I don't understand the thing what he said.
questionBank.correctAnswer: I don't understand what he said.
questionBank.explanation: 'What' acts as the noun itself. If you add 'reason' or 'words', you must use 'that'.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The information what you provided was extremely helpful.'
questionBank.explanation: The noun 'information' is already present, so 'that' or 'which' is required.

questionBank.question: Complete the sentence: 'Everything ___ he touched turned to gold.'
questionBank.explanation: With words like 'everything', 'something', or 'all', we use 'that'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct version: 'Is this the gift ___ you wanted?'
questionBank.explanation: 'Gift' is a noun, blocking the use of 'what'.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The man who he called was my brother.'
questionBank.explanation: This is a 'double subject' error. 'Who' already acts as the object/link. Correct: 'The man who I called' (if the subject is I) or 'The man he called'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct pronoun: '___ you need is a good night's sleep.'
questionBank.explanation: No noun precedes the gap, so 'What' (the thing that) is the subject.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'He told me the secret what I promised not to share.'
questionBank.explanation: Should be 'that' because 'secret' is the antecedent.

questionBank.question: Which is correct? 'I found the keys ___ I lost yesterday.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Keys' is the noun being described.

questionBank.question: Complete: 'They didn't give me ___ I asked for.'
questionBank.explanation: 'What' is a shortcut for 'the thing that'.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The report what was on the desk is gone.'
questionBank.explanation: Use 'that' or 'which' after the noun 'report'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct pronoun: 'I don't remember the name of the hotel ___ we stayed.'
questionBank.explanation: Refers to a location.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'This is the book what I bought for the class.'
questionBank.explanation: Incorrect relative pronoun after a noun.

questionBank.question: Which is correct? '___ she said was quite surprising.'
questionBank.explanation: Acts as the subject meaning 'The thing that she said'.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The restaurant what we went to was excellent.'
questionBank.explanation: Should be 'that' or 'which'.

questionBank.question: Complete: 'Did you hear ___ happened at the meeting?'
questionBank.explanation: No noun precedes the gap.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The advice what my father gave me was helpful.'
questionBank.explanation: Advice is the noun; 'that' is the correct linker.

questionBank.question: Which is correct? 'I'll do ___ I can to help.'
questionBank.explanation: 'All' is the noun/pronoun here, so 'that' is the linker.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The students what were late missed the quiz.'
questionBank.explanation: For people, use 'who' or 'that'.

questionBank.question: Complete: 'I can't believe ___ he did.'
questionBank.explanation: Standard use of a nominal relative clause.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The movie what we saw was very long.'
questionBank.explanation: Should be 'that' or 'which'.

questionBank.question: Which is correct? 'Tell me ___ you are thinking about.'
questionBank.explanation: No noun before the gap.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The car what I drive is ten years old.'
questionBank.explanation: Incorrect relative pronoun after 'car'.

questionBank.question: Complete: 'Please show me ___ you bought.'
questionBank.explanation: Acts as the object of 'show'.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The story what she told me was unbelievable.'
questionBank.explanation: Should be 'that' or 'which'.

questionBank.question: Which is correct? 'I don't know ___ to do.'
questionBank.explanation: Question-word relative pronoun.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The plan what we discussed is perfect.'
questionBank.explanation: Should be 'that' or 'which'.

questionBank.question: Complete: 'This is precisely ___ I was talking about.'
questionBank.explanation: Acts as a nominal relative pronoun.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The reason what I am late is traffic.'
questionBank.explanation: Should be 'that' or 'why'.

questionBank.question: Final Logic Check: In English, can you have a Noun + What?
questionBank.explanation: 'What' already contains the noun 'thing,' so it cannot follow another noun.

levels.title: Contextual Correction: The Comma Rule
levels.miniLesson:
📝 Level 3: Essential vs. Extra Info

Relative clauses come in two types. The difference is all about whether the information is essential to identify the noun or just extra information.

  • 🎯 Defining (No Commas): The students who studied passed the test. (Only the ones who studied).
  • 💡 Non-defining (With Commas): The students, who studied, passed the test. (All the students passed, and by the way, they all studied).
  • ⚠️ The 'THAT' Rule: You can never use 'that' in a non-defining clause (the one with commas). Use 'which' for things and 'who' for people.

questionBank.question: Which sentence implies I have more than one brother? 'My brother who lives in Tokyo is a doctor.'
options: Neither implies I have more than one.
Both imply I have more than one.
questionBank.explanation: No commas mean the clause 'who lives in Tokyo' is essential to distinguish *which* brother I am talking about, implying there are others.

questionBank.question: Identify the punctuation error: 'Paris, that is the capital of France, is very beautiful.'
questionBank.explanation: You cannot use 'that' in a non-defining clause (one set off by commas). It should be 'which'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct punctuation: 'The house ___ has a red door is mine.'
questionBank.explanation: This is a defining clause (identifying which house), so no commas are needed, and either 'which' or 'that' is fine.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'Albert Einstein who developed the theory of relativity was a genius.'
questionBank.explanation: Einstein's name already identifies him; the clause is extra info and needs commas.

questionBank.question: Which sentence is correct for 'extra' information?
options: My car that is ten years old still works.
My car, which is ten years old, still works.
My car which is ten years old still works.
My car, that is ten years old, still works.
questionBank.correctAnswer: My car, which is ten years old, still works.
questionBank.explanation: Non-defining clauses use 'which' and commas.

questionBank.question: What is the meaning? 'The workers, who went on strike, were fired.'
questionBank.explanation: The commas imply that 'who went on strike' applies to the entire group of workers mentioned.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The book, that I bought yesterday, is very interesting.'
questionBank.explanation: You cannot use 'that' after a comma in a relative clause.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct form: 'Vegetables ___ are grown organically are more expensive.'
questionBank.explanation: This defines *which* vegetables (only organic ones), so no commas are used.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'Tokyo which is the capital of Japan is a huge city.'
questionBank.explanation: Since there is only one Tokyo, the description is extra info and needs commas.

questionBank.question: Which relative pronoun is used for 'extra' info about a person?
questionBank.explanation: In non-defining clauses (with commas), we use 'who' for people.

questionBank.question: Meaning check: 'The windows which were broken have been fixed.'
options: Only the broken windows were fixed.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Only the broken windows were fixed.
questionBank.explanation: No commas = Defining clause. It identifies a specific subset of windows.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'My mother, that is a lawyer, lives in New York.'
questionBank.explanation: Non-defining clauses must use 'who' for people.

questionBank.question: Which sentence implies I have only ONE sister? 'My sister, who is a nurse, lives in Kyoto.'
options: Neither implies I have one sister.
questionBank.explanation: Commas show the info is extra; I don't need it to 'define' which sister because I likely only have one.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The report which I finished it yesterday is on the desk.'
questionBank.explanation: Redundant pronoun. 'Which' already replaces 'it'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct punctuation: 'London ___ which is the capital of the UK ___ is very old.'
questionBank.explanation: Proper nouns always take non-defining clauses with commas.

questionBank.question: Which pronoun is forbidden after a comma?
questionBank.explanation: 'That' is only for defining clauses without commas.

questionBank.question: Meaning check: 'The athletes, who had trained hard, won medals.'
options: All the athletes won and they all worked hard.
questionBank.correctAnswer: All the athletes won and they all worked hard.
questionBank.explanation: The commas make 'who had trained hard' a description of the whole group.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The man, who his car was stolen, called the police.'
questionBank.explanation: Should be 'whose car' for possession.

questionBank.question: Which fits a defining clause for a thing?
questionBank.explanation: In defining clauses for things, 'that' and 'which' are interchangeable.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'My cat, that I've had for five years, is very lazy.'
questionBank.explanation: Cannot use 'that' in a non-defining clause.

questionBank.question: Meaning check: 'The cars which are electric are cheaper to run.'
options: All cars are cheaper to run.
Only electric cars are cheaper to run.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Only electric cars are cheaper to run.
questionBank.explanation: No commas define a specific subset of 'cars'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct punctuation: 'My parents ___ who live in Canada ___ are coming to visit.'
questionBank.explanation: 'My parents' is a specific group; the location is extra info.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The phone, which I bought it last week, is already broken.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Which' replaces 'it'. Don't use both.

questionBank.question: Which is correct for a non-defining person clause?
questionBank.explanation: Non-defining requires commas and 'who'.

questionBank.question: Meaning check: 'The books, which were on the top shelf, were dusty.'
options: Only books on the top shelf were dusty.
All the books were on the top shelf and they were all dusty.
questionBank.correctAnswer: All the books were on the top shelf and they were all dusty.
questionBank.explanation: Commas make the location extra info for the whole group.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'William Shakespeare who was born in Stratford wrote Hamlet.'
questionBank.explanation: Missing comma after Shakespeare (non-defining).

questionBank.question: Which is correct? 'The reason ___ I am late is a secret.'
questionBank.explanation: 'The reason why' and 'the reason that' are both standard.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The hotel, that we stayed in, was very nice.'
questionBank.explanation: Cannot use 'that' after a comma.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct pronoun: 'She is the woman ___ daughter won the race.'
questionBank.explanation: Shows the possession link (the daughter of the woman).

questionBank.question: Final Check: Non-defining clauses ___ the meaning of the noun.
questionBank.explanation: Defining clauses limit/define; non-defining clauses just add extra info.

levels.title: IELTS/TOEFL Integration
levels.miniLesson:
📈 Level 4: The Data-Nesting Technique

In academic writing, relative clauses are used to add precision and evidence without starting new, simple sentences. This increases your Lexical Density and Grammatical Range.

  • 📊 The Data Nest: The percentage of students, which peaked at 40% in 2010, declined steadily thereafter.
  • 🧠 The Sentential 'Which': Use 'which' after a comma to comment on a whole preceding idea. The population grew rapidly, which led to a housing shortage.
  • 🏛️ Formal Prepositions: In high-level register, place the preposition before the pronoun. The era in which he lived... (instead of 'The era he lived in').

questionBank.question: Which sentence is the best Task 1 overview for a graph showing 2010 data?
options: The figure for coal, which was 50 tonnes in 2010, rose to 80 tonnes by 2020.
The figure for coal which was 50 tonnes in 2010 rose to 80 tonnes by 2020.
The figure for coal, that was 50 tonnes in 2010, rose to 80 tonnes by 2020.
The figure for coal, what was 50 tonnes in 2010, rose to 80 tonnes by 2020.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The figure for coal, which was 50 tonnes in 2010, rose to 80 tonnes by 2020.
questionBank.explanation: This uses a non-defining relative clause with commas to nest a specific data point into a trend sentence.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The unemployment rate fell to 5% in 2015. Which was the lowest point in the decade.'
questionBank.explanation: This is a fragment. 'Which' must be attached to the previous sentence with a comma to refer back to the fact mentioned.

questionBank.question: Choose the most formal academic structure:
options: The method which the results were obtained by is controversial.
The method by which the results were obtained is controversial.
The method what the results were obtained by is controversial.
The method that the results were obtained by is controversial.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The method by which the results were obtained is controversial.
questionBank.explanation: Placing the preposition ('by') before the relative pronoun ('which') is the hallmark of a high academic register.

questionBank.question: Which sentence correctly uses 'whose' for an inanimate object in a Task 1 context?
options: The sector whose growth was most significant was technology.
The sector who growth was most significant was technology.
The sector which growth was most significant was technology.
The sector that growth was most significant was technology.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The sector whose growth was most significant was technology.
questionBank.explanation: 'Whose' can be used as a possessive for both people and inanimate objects (the growth of the sector).

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The two countries, that shared a border, had very different economic outcomes.'
questionBank.explanation: 'That' cannot be used in non-defining clauses (those with commas). Use 'which'.

questionBank.question: Complete the Task 2 argument: 'Governments should invest in renewable energy, ___ would reduce carbon emissions.'
questionBank.explanation: A sentential 'which' is used after a comma to refer to the entire idea of investing in renewable energy.

questionBank.question: Which is correct for a 'reduced' relative clause? 'The participants ___ the survey were all under 30.'
questionBank.explanation: This is a reduced relative clause (short for 'who were completing' or 'who completed').

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The city in that I was born has changed significantly.'
questionBank.explanation: You cannot use 'that' after a preposition. It must be 'in which'.

questionBank.question: Which sentence effectively uses 'whom' in a formal context?
options: The people to who the survey was sent responded quickly.
The people to whom the survey was sent responded quickly.
The people whom the survey was sent responded quickly.
The people who the survey was sent to whom responded quickly.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The people to whom the survey was sent responded quickly.
questionBank.explanation: 'Whom' is the object form and follows the preposition 'to'.

questionBank.question: Identify the punctuation error: 'The report, which was published in 2022 provided new insights.'
questionBank.explanation: Missing a second comma after '2022' to close the non-defining clause.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct pronoun: 'The reason ___ the project failed was a lack of funding.'
questionBank.explanation: 'The reason why' or 'The reason that' are both acceptable in academic writing.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The data suggests a trend, what is likely to continue.'
questionBank.explanation: 'What' cannot be a relative pronoun for a noun. Use 'which'.

questionBank.question: Which sentence is correctly 'nested' with data?
options: Wheat production, at 20 tons in 2000, fell to 10 tons in 2010.
Wheat production which was 20 tons in 2000 fell to 10 tons in 2010.
Wheat production, that was 20 tons in 2000, fell to 10 tons in 2010.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Wheat production, at 20 tons in 2000, fell to 10 tons in 2010.
questionBank.explanation: Using a prepositional phrase (at 20 tons) is a very common 'reduced' way to nest data in Task 1.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'There are many situations in where this rule does not apply.'
questionBank.explanation: Should be 'situations in which' or 'situations where'. 'In where' is incorrect.

questionBank.question: Which fits a formal opening for Task 2? 'The extent ___ technology affects daily life is immense.'
questionBank.explanation: The noun 'extent' is almost always paired with the preposition 'to' (to an extent).

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The graph, whose data comes from the World Bank, shows a rise in GDP.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Whose' is perfectly correct for referring to the data belonging to the graph.

questionBank.question: Choose the best pronoun: 'The criteria ___ the candidates were judged were strict.'
questionBank.explanation: Candidates are judged 'by' criteria, so 'by which' is the formal choice.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The professor, that I spoke to yesterday, was very helpful.'
questionBank.explanation: Cannot use 'that' after a comma. Use 'who' or 'whom'.

questionBank.question: Which sentence correctly uses a sentential relative clause?
options: The company went bankrupt, which was a shock to everyone.
The company went bankrupt. Which was a shock to everyone.
The company went bankrupt which was a shock to everyone.
The company went bankrupt, that was a shock to everyone.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The company went bankrupt, which was a shock to everyone.
questionBank.explanation: Requires a comma and 'which' to comment on the whole previous clause.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The town in where the festival is held is very small.'
questionBank.explanation: Should be 'the town where' or 'the town in which'.

questionBank.question: Choose the most accurate Task 1 phrasing:
options: Sales of cars, which stood at 100 units, then rose to 150.
Sales of cars, stood at 100 units, then rose to 150.
Sales of cars which stood at 100 units then rose to 150.
Sales of cars, that stood at 100 units, then rose to 150.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Sales of cars, which stood at 100 units, then rose to 150.
questionBank.explanation: Correct use of a non-defining clause to provide a baseline data point.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'He is the only person that I know who can speak six languages.'
questionBank.explanation: This sentence correctly uses 'that' for the first restriction and 'who' for the second.

questionBank.question: Which is more formal? 'The theory that he is famous for' or 'The theory for which he is famous'?
questionBank.explanation: Preposition-first structure (Pied-piping) is the academic standard.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The data, what was collected over ten years, shows a trend.'
questionBank.explanation: Should be 'which'.

questionBank.question: Complete the phrase: 'The conditions ___ the workers lived were terrible.'
questionBank.explanation: Workers live 'under' conditions; this is the formal relative structure.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'None of the students, which were invited, attended the event.'
questionBank.explanation: For students (people), use 'who'.

questionBank.question: Which is correct? 'I don't know the person ___ you are talking.'
questionBank.explanation: Formal object form with the required preposition.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'The city that I live in it is very crowded.'
questionBank.explanation: Redundant pronoun. 'That' already replaces 'it'. Should be 'The city that I live in'.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct pronoun: 'The results ___ the researchers found were surprising.'
questionBank.explanation: Refers to 'results' (things).

questionBank.question: Final Check: In Task 1, relative clauses help to ___.
options: Increase word count without adding value.
Combine data points and trends into single, complex sentences.
Sound more like a story than a report.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Combine data points and trends into single, complex sentences.
questionBank.explanation: This is the primary goal of using relative clauses in data description.
